. CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Department and the I.A.O.8. got some rather
~ severo handling in Limerick recently. In those days of
. the whitewashing of Sir Horace, it is the fashion to
- represent, that the outcry against the Department is a
- mere ignorant, howl that cannot stand the test of exami-
ation. We have no doubt that the element of ignorant
owling is, to some extent, present in the volume of
rse opinion concerning the Department; but the
co of that element in no way acquits the Depart-
or whitewashes Sir Horace. Mr. A. W. Shaw,
‘Thomas Cleeve, and Alderman O’Meara, of
big men in the Irish industrial world,
arcely be brushed aside with a superior

, howlers at Sir Horace Plunkett.

At a meeting of the members of the Limerick
Chamber of Commerce, Mr. A. W. Shaw, a personal
friend of Sir Horace Plunkett, declared that much of
the bad trade in the bacon industry was due to the fact
that that industry, instead of being fostered and
encouraged by the Board of Agriculture, had been
almost destroyed.  The I.A.O.S.—which, until recent
revelations, Mr. Shaw thought was a private association
supported by voluntary contributions—was now known
to have been heavily subsidised by the Department, and
was really a branch of the Board of Agriculture. Said
Mr. Shaw : —“ With the money they got from the Board
of Agriculture they started a crusade by paid men, who
went through the country agitating the country against
the Munster provision trade. He was in a position to
tell them that one of those who preached that doctrine
was absolutely in the pay of the Board of Agriculture.’’
Mr. Shaw complained also that when an attack was
made on Irish bacon by the Danish Consulate, it was
left to a number of Irish firms in London to repel it.
and the Board of Agriculture did nothing.  That,”
said Mr. Shaw, “ was a sin of omission on the part of
the Board of Agriculture, whose sins of commission were
very considerable.’”’

Sir Thomas Cleeve’s firm pays £50,000 a year in wages,
50 he is no irresponsible flinger of epithets at the Depart-
ment. He said that his story was very much the same
as that of Mr. Shaw, only that his complaints concerned
butter, and not bacon. Sir Thomas Cleeve had been
mixed up with the co-operative movement from its early
stages. He had thought that it would concern itself
with outlying districts, but instead of that its anxiety
was to attack existing industries. Sir Thomas said :—
“ They said they were fostering present industries, and
encouraging new ones. What fostering had they done?
So far, it had been against them (the butter trade), and
if they knocked them under, the miller or someone else’s
turn would come next. He thought the Commission
that was going on was altogether a one-sided business.”’

It is significant that Mr. Plews, the Manager of the
Great Northern Bigot Railway, declared that the
recent conference between the Irish railway mis-
managers and the deputation organised by the Transit
Committeo should be held in private.  The railways
don’t like the light. The question of through rates, to
which so much attention was drawn some years ago in
the LEADER, naturally cropped up. Mr. Furlong
stated thab Irish firms did not want preferential rates;
that they merely asked that Irish manufacturers should
bo able to send their goods from one part of Ireland to
another at as low a rate as the rate given to cross-
Channel and foreign competitors. The innocent Mr. '
Plows asked what proof there was that this condition of
affairs did not exist at the present time. What a ques-
tion to ask, to be sure.  The question discloses the
quibbling spirit in which men like Mr. Plews view these
matters. It was a new thing, and perhaps in the esti-
mation of Mr. Plews an uncalled-for thing, that repre-
sentations on the part of low-down Irish manufactures
should be made to high-up railway managers like Mr.
Plows and Napoleon of Kingsbridge, the organiser of
“sick” traffics and depreciated capital values.

Mr. Plews made two statements that conflict with
cach other. He said that he thought investigation
would prove that Irish railways did not carry imported
goods at a lower rato than Irish goods, and he said also
that if Irish railways carried Irish goods at the same
figure as imported goods coming into Ireland that the
companies would soon be in the Bankruptcy Court.

Both these views cannot be correct. As for the latter,
it shows an extraordinary attitude. If foreign dumps
‘are not given preferential rates over Irish-made goods
the railways would go bankrupt, so that to keep the
railways out of bankruptcy foreign dumpers must be
encouraged and Irish manufactures penalised! Surely
10 one but an Irish railway director could take a view
like that. It is a policy of feeding Irish railways on
artificial stimulants that sap the vitality of the country.
But perhaps in view of the manipulation of tho capital
account on the Great Sourface Railway we might not
be surprised at this other phase of self-revelation of the
Irish railway mind. It appears that the canny Scot
has no “ through rate ”’ into or out of his native land.
The public are at a great disadvantage concerning this
conference, as the Press was not admitted; however,
perhaps it is a wonder that such great people as Mr.
Plews, and Dent the Napoleon of “sick ’ traffies, con-
descended to meet plain people engaged in trade and
industry at all.

These notes were in type on Monday when we read
an explanatory letter from Mr. Plews in the Freceman.
The statement attributed to the gallant Lieutenant-
Colonel, “that if the Irish Railway Companies carried
goods at the same figure as imported goods coming into
Ireland at competitive rates, then the companies would
soon be in the Bankruptcy Court,”” is not correct, it
appears. The gallant manager “made no such general
assertion, nor would such a statement as it appears in
this report be in accordance with the facts.””  This
disclaimer of the gallant manager smells very strongly
of diplomacy. Will the gallant colonel tell the public
exactly what statement he did make? This all comes
of hugger-mugger meetings. Why were not the Press
allowed into the conference? A soldier, even a toy one
like the Lieutenant-Colonel, surely ought to be direct
and precise in speech.

The Methodists were in conference in Belfast, and,

needless to say, bold, bad “ Rome ’’ got it hard. Bishop

Burt—it is the first time, we must confess, that we

heard of a Methodist bishop—harangued the conference

in the Grosvenor Hall. In the course of his harangue,

as reported in the Belfast News Letter, Bishop Burt

declared :—‘ There were marvellous possibilities before

the country if the yoke of Rome could be broken and

the English Government would learn some lessons of

wisdom. The day would come, for the general revolt

against Rome would some bright morning arrive in Ire-

land, and then they would all rejoice. He was glad to

report progress and success in all departments of their

- great Church.”” Referring to “Rome’’ in the United

[ States, Bishop Burt remarked:—“It was said that

2 Romanism was making great progress in the United

& States. They were sorry to confess that the members of

I that Church were making great gains politically and

financially, but numerically and in proportion to immi-

gration they were not progressing.’””  The italics are

ours. So then Bishop Burt and his like are sorry for

the financial success of Catholics in America.  From

this open expression of sorrow we may take it that men

of that kind would hit “ Rome ’’ if they could by retard-

ing the financial prospects of “Idolators.”” And what

of the audience who listened to him at Belfast? Well,

none of them have much reason to regret the financial

progress of “Idolators’’ in the service of the Great

Northern Railway, anyway. The “ non-sectarian’’

management look after that, Later down in his

harangue, Bishop Burt said :— 8till, Romanism was a

great problem, principally because Methodist ministers

and people did not understand the institution.” We

do not see how it follows that it is any greater a problem

because Methodists don’t understand it; but, anyway,

ag admittedly they don’t understand it, why rant or rave
about it in Belfast?

It is as well to look at these matters fairly and
squarely; doing so may free us from many a blunder
and foolish notion. Here in one of the strongholds of 1
the Gacl, where Irish is largely spoken, the representa-
tives of the people elected a non-Irish-speaking doctor
in preference to an Irish-speaking one. The day of the
dominance of Irish Ireland is by no means ““no far
distant *’ ; it is very much distant; these are still only
the days of early campaigning, and it is well to keep
that fact in our recollections. Naturally enough some
enthusiastics think that victory is already upon their
banners, and that they can dictate terms. It is well,
perhaps, to have that type abroad, though some of the
type, if they lack tact, may be greater powers for re-
action than for progress.

The Ulster Guardian is a Protestant paper, and in its
issue of June 23rd there is a contribution from a Pro-
testant who signs himself ‘ Cosmopolite.”” The contri-
bution is headed “Civil and Religious Liberty in
Magheragall.””  “ Cosmopolite ’ appears to be an in-
dividual Protestant of the non-bitter type. He was
invited to a social entertainment arranged by the prin-
cipal teacher of Ballyclough “ National ’’ school. The
entertainment was in connection with some Catholic
fund or other, and  Cosmopolite’’ went there in
response to the invitation. There were other Protes-
tants there also. The proceedings commenced with a
concert, and subsequently there was dancing on the
lawn before the school door. We quote from the letter
of this Protestant correspondent:—

I hoard the beating of drums in the distance, and shortly after
o number of men and boys assembled on the road opposite the
school. I thought at first they were friends or servants of those
inside, but it soon became apparent that their presence was not of a.

there camo somo vile and insulting phrases; then a verse or two
of some Orango ditly referring to how the Popo should be treated;
then that old, old war ory, cursing tho Pope; then the wilder

any more dancing. Somo retired into the sohool, others took

by vilo opithets and insulting abuse no responso in word or deed

that fino phrase associated with tho loving qualifications of an
Orangeman repeated in a July anniversury sormon. e

reaction?  The recent resolution ordering that all

The first article in the cwrrent number of the New
Ireland Review is called “Intolerant Democracy.”’ It
is by J. H. Hutchinson, B.A., a son of the ex-Lord
Mayor of Dublin, we presume. It is a vigorous and
courageous article, and suggests originality. It is a
type of article that is to be welcomed whether one fully
agrees with it or not. It goes to show that Democracy
(with the big. D) and other forces in Ireland may have
to face a logical test from thinking young Ireland.
Democracy (with the big D) has sometimes one great
advantage. It knows so little that it can be cock-sure
of a great deal. The more a man knows and under-
stands, the more cautious he is in coming to conclu-
sions.  Democracy frequently rushes to conclusions,
and the silver Iming to that cloud is that rushing to
conclusions inevitably creates violent reactions.

Mr. Hutchinson treads on the toes of Demos in some
tart sayings for which he may get “back answers.”
For instance, he remarks—‘To retain the confidence
and support of the masses, you must never rise above
them.”” And again, “To hint for a moment that the
evils from which the masses suffer are not wholly due
to class oppression, is an act of treason to the people’s
cause that cannot be forgiven.”” And yet again, “No
regular newspaper reader can have failed to note how
the tone of public debate has been lowered, not only in
Great Britain, but also in our Irish cities, sinco the
advent of the ‘popular’ element to administrative
bodies.””  Mr. Hutchinson also says:—Truculent
demeanour and vulgarity are weapons thoe use of which
always achioves a certain popularity with the crowd.”
Again :— Finally, your present-day democrat cannot,
or will not, distinguish between party loyalty and party
slavery.”” Mr, Hutchinson has undoubtedly a sonse of
humour. For many reasons we appreciate the follow-
ing :— The humour of the situation froquently lies in
the fact that your neighbour, stoutly opposed to every
part of the popular programme, does nob bring upon
himself one-tenth of the denunciations to which you are
subjected.”  Truly the author says—*A democratic
' halo may decorate tyranny, bub it cannob sanctify ib.’

The Galway Board of Guardians recently were con-
fronted with the problem of choosing a medical doctor
in Galway No. 3 Dispensary, which comprises the Aran
Islands. There were three applicants, and whctherlor
not any of them knew Irish we cannot say. The TIrish
question does not appear to have been raised during the
election. One of the applications came from the county
of Sligo, and when it was read Mr. Martin Ryan rose
and said: —‘“ Away with him, we won’t have him ; let
him go back to Belmullet or Sligo, or wherever the
devil he came from.” Evidently Mr. Martin Ryan was
labouring under an acute attack of Galway spirit
stimulated by the mention of Sligo.  ‘“Isn’t he an
Irishman,”” asked a brother Guardian. Mr. Ryan re-
torted: —“ Who knows what he is? Te may be a
Iottentot for all I know, and while I represent the
Claddagh, T’ll protest against bringing in a stranger
while we have men of our own.” And a local news-
paper reporter informs us that—‘ Whilst making this
pronouncement Mr. Ryan beat time on the table in
front of him, and looked every inch like a man that
meant what he said.”” But apparently Mr. Ryan had
been in the Militia, for Mr. Grealish expressed a doubt
that anyone who had been in the Militia could claim to
be an Irishman. Mr. Ryan admitted the impeachment,
and said that he could ‘‘lead an army from behind
equal to Napoleon or Wellington or Roberts, or any
of them; but he protested against any outside doctor
being brought to Aran.”’

Father McDonald, the Administrator of Westport,
contributes another letter to the A/ayo News concerning
tho Rev. Mr. Hannay, the bigot of Westport. In it
he deals with Mr. Hannay’s libels on the Foxford in-
dustry. A character in Mr. Hannay’s libellous book
meets a crowd of emigrants in a train. One of them, a
girl, tells him that she is glad to be leaving Ireland,
for there was nothing to be earned in the factory, that
she was the best worker the Nuns had, that she worked
for three years, and at the end of that time all she was
getting was six shillings a week; she also said that
many a girl worked there, and had no more than one-
and-nincpence to take home with her at the end of the
week. The very mention of exact sums of shillings and
pence lends extra poison to this libel, for it gives an
impression to the readers that the libeller is writing
from minute and first hand information.  Father
McDonald, the Administrator of Westport, thus deals
with this part of the tale of the bigot of that town:—

This was so damaging to the Nuns and their factory that
I went specially to Foxford to ascertain the facts. I had never
scon the factory before. Its extent and wonderful machinery
surprised me. I had often heard at a railway station half a
dozen steam engines hissing at once, but my first impression of
tho factory was a hundred engines all belching together, loud and
impatient. I was shown through every part. I watched the
various looms at work. I saw tweeds and serges and blankets
and ‘flannels and friczes and rugs being woven. I saw the
hosicry where knittod articles of every description are turned out
by the hundred. I saw the yarn room with three thousand.
pounds worth of yarn ready for tho loom. I saw mountains
upon mountains of beautiful fleeces all grown in Mayo. And 1
saw tho warerooms with soveral thousand pounds worth of finished
matorial, in fabrics for every purpose, and in every colour, shade,
and tint that Irish wool can assume. But my chief purpose was
to ascerlain tho earnings of tho workers. Thero are 130 hands
employed. I onquired hero and there, at random, the wages
paid to this boy and that girl. According to my notes I made
onquiries about fourteen porsons, and their weekly wages in shil-
lings aro as follows:—12, 16, 16, 10, 18, 14, 30, 12, 8, 22, 11, 20, 10,
16. Somo of these are only learners. Tho accounts are audited
twico o year. Tho amount paid.in wages last year was only
cloven pounds short of being five thousand, and anybody can
obsorve that tho littlo town has an air of prosperity not to be
notioed in other towns. In tho faco of all theso faots and figures
I would like to ask what motive can bo attributed to Mr. Hannay
for writing in his book that ‘“the best worker they had was
gotting only six shillings o week, that othors got only one and
nineponoo, and that no ono got but starvation wages” ?

We know tha.t it is 1·egarded by some people as 
'' stirring up sectarian strife " for an " Idolator " to 
stand up for Tolerance, Justice, and Fair Play, while 
we suppose a Protestant minister, who no doubt draws 
a big salary out of the enormous annual income which 
is drained from the economic wealth of the country by 
the disestablished but highly financed church, may pose 
as a sort of a Nationalist and turn round and libel even 
women with impunity. 
• 
At the Post Office Inquiry, Mr. Sanderson, of the 
Dublin Sorting Department, was asked a few questions. 
Mr. Meehan wanted to know why juniors were pushed 
on over the heads of seniors in the matter of promotion. 
'.' Am I right," he asked, "in presuming that promotion 
1s brought about by favouritism 1" Of course, Mr. 0. C. 
San~erson, of the Dublin Sorting Office, denied the 
soft impeachment. 
The officers are judged on the per-
formance 0f their duties, he said, and there was no 
difference made in connection with religious faith. 
Of 
course not; there never is, not even on the Great 
Northern Railway! 
The public has not yet been favoured with the pro-
mised balance-sheet 0£ the All-Ireland Temperance 
Bazaar. 
The public know, however, th11.t the paid 
secretary of the Irish Association for the Prevention of 
Intemperance, Mr. Moriarty, has gone bankrupt with a 
vengeance-if we remember aright the state of affairs 
was £2,100 liabilities and £200 assets. 
He has since, 
we understand, severed his connection with the Associa-
tion. 
In view of Mr. Moriarty's. smash, we think the 
bazaar people ought to make all the more hurry in 
getting out the audited accounts. 
What is being done with all the money realised at the 
bazaar? Our readers will remember that we supplied 
thein with tidings of a music teacher who was giving 
lectures somewhere in the midlands. 
That little enter-
prise would be connected with the Irish Association for 
J;he Prevention of Intemperance. 
We have before us a 
circular from "Yow·s very truly, Wm. Wilkinson," 
secretary of the "Irish Temperance League," of Bel-
fast. 
This League, we understand, got half, or is to 
get half, of the profits of the All-Ireland Temperance 
Bazaar. It runs an official organ which last year con-
tained an article that had a most insulting reference 
to the Catholic religion, although the Irish Temperance 
~eague is supposed to be what is styled "non-sectarian." 
The circular from Wm. Wilkinson is printed in green 
ink on paper with the ,vater-mark " Government Fine." 
Now, this circular contains particulars- of a "Tem-
perance Summer School '' that, presumably out of I-he 
funds of the All-Ireland Bazaar, is to be run in Port-
rush. 
Judging by the particulars and names given, we 
should say t.hat the whole thing is un-Irish if not anti-
Irish and decidedly Sourface. 
There are to be lectures 
'on, amongst other subjects, music and elocution. 
Now, 
what have these to do directly with Temperance Re-
form 7 Did the people who spent their money at the 
All-Ireland Bazaar do _so with any view to adding to 
the musical and oratorical accomplishments of the 13el-
fast pars0ns and others of that class who are likely io 
attend this "Temperance Summer School" in Port-
rush 7 
We read in the circular:-" On ~onday 
evening there will be a Reception in the Town Hall, 
on Wednesday afternoon a Reception in the Cafe, Oole:-
raine, on Friday afternoon a Garden Party at Lord 
Macnaghten's, Runkerry, and on other afternoons 
Gipsy Teas and Excursions will be arranged." 
Is this 
the way to spend the money of the All-Ireland Bazaar 1 
We never heard of Lord Macnaghten before, but we 
confess we got a mild start when we read of a garden 
party at Runkerry-it looks and sounds so very like 
Drunkery. 
Board and lodging for the week is only to cost from 
2Is., and we note that the house will be open from 1st 
August. 
Will these terms pay, or is the board and 
lodging part of the scheme to be run partly at the 
expense of. the fund a of the '' Irish Temperance 
Leaguef" If the latter, is it equitable to run a &how 
like tha.t out of other people'~ money1 
Oheap holidays 
at the seaside are all very well, but o~ what principle 
do the authorities of the Irish Temperance League 
justify a. home for people like parsons and their ladies

CORRESPONDENCE.

THRESHINGS AND DRINK.

Sir,—Your paper has done so much for Temperance
in Ireland, that I think it only right to bring under
your notice a disgraceful custom that oxists in all, or
nearly all, corn-growing districts in Ireland. It is
the abominable custom of supplying drink to workers
at threshing machines.

At these threshings there are possibly 20 men em-
ployed, usually 20 to 25, and there are always a few
young chaps just in their teens, just the boys with their
Confirmation pledges still unbroken. As a rule there
is a keg or two of porter, never less, a couple of bottles
of whiskey and sometimes minerals for the teetotallers.

Anyway there is always sufficient to have nearly all
the workers drunk, and what is worse still, young fel-
lows are enticed to forget their Confirmation pledges,
with the result that before the threshing season is out
many of these chaps are known to be “drinkin’,”” and
I know it for a fact that in very few cases do any of
them ever dream of renewing their Confirmation pledge
once they have broken it in this manner.

But the strangest part of this business is that in a
district where nearly all the farmers are Catholics, and
where there are but one or two Protestant families,
who have as a consequence to depend upon their Ca-
= B tholic neighbours to help them at their threshings, the
g Catholics work for the Protestant farmers without any

drink, for none is offered them, whereas if a Catholic
farmer were to act that way towards them—why, they’d

;, go on strike. Now, is not that a sad state of affairs!

REeADING Books 1N NATIONAL SCHOOLS.

Dear Sir,—Now that the school year is about to
commence, I desire to draw attention to the important
subject of selecting suitable “readers’’ for use by the
pupils. The matter is practically in the hands of the
managers; and they must blame themselves if they
do not use the powers invested in them. There are
many such books in the market that are not suitable;
and sometimes objectionable books have been in use.

I hope that books of practical utility will not be
omitted in the selection of new books. What subject
is more practical in Ireland than that of temperance?
We have, I am glad to say, some readers on this head,
sanctioned by the Board; and some of them high'y
recommended by Irish Bishops. May we not hope that
such “readers’’ will be soon in use in every school in
Ireland—primary and intermediate, and in our colleges
and convents?—Yours faithfully,

MR. BUNG COMPROMISES.

N accerdance with the compromise the bungeries in
the exempted cities where Bung’s Bond Slaves have
to toil on Sundays from two to seven will only have to
work from two to five. Two hours extra leave on Sun-
days is a great boon to those of Bung’s Bond Slaves who
work in bungeries in the exempted cities. The bona
limit is to be six miles, and for that reform all honest
and disinterested men will be thankful. The “ Sheds’’
of Clontarf, and the bungeries of some of the Patriots
of Blackrock, no doubt, are annoyed at this arrange-
ment. As for the down-trodden bogus-bona, his sorrows
are worthy of being set forth in song. We wonder does
the “Cuckoo,”” the owner of which interested himself
in the testimonial to the Tallaght policeman, come
within the six mile limit? Perhaps the bogus “ bonas
will be buying bicycles now! If they do, we hope, at
least, that they will purchase machines of Irish make.
But in view of the necessity of coming back from, as
well as going to, the bungery six miles away, perhaps
the prudent “bona ’’ would consider a tricycle a safer
investment.

The closing of bungeries an howr earlier on Saturday
nights should be an increased blessing. In practice,
the public-houses rather than the sun register night-
fall in town. As soon as Mr. Bung puts out his lights
all, teetotalers and drinkers alike, feel that it is late,
and time that they were at home. In Dublin it will be
a groat thing to have Bung shut up at ten o’clock on
Saturday nights; other shops, such as popular drapery
places and the like, will, no doubt, respond in sympathy
to the closing of Bung, and their overworked assistants
may look forward as a consequence of this Bung Bill to a
shortening of their hours on Saturday nights.

Certainly the Temperance Party left Bung down
lightly. Since the Leaper started a great change has
come over Irish public opinion in relation to Mr. Bung’s
position in this nation. Bung isno fool, and he quickly
made up his mind that it were better for him to offer
terms rather than let the Bill in its original form be
fought out. He offered terms, and they have been ac-
copted, but there is certainly no reason to gush over
them. By offering terms Bung probably gained the
right to open for three hours in the exempted cities, he
got an hour added on to his Saturday night in the big
towns, and he got a mile off the march of the thirsty
bogus “ bona.” \

Mr. Bung is very often a decent fellow personally,
and he would be a good patriot if he could fll that o

out hurt to his “takings.”” ~ Wo flatter ourselves
that we rubbed a lot of the gilt off the Bung trade. How-
- ever, we would bo the last to make a scape-goat of Bung.
- If Bung swelled on the nation’s thirst, tho thirst in
<quity should bear its share of the blame. Bung went
into the bungery trade because it paid, and because
there wero very fow other trades open. The Bung trado

supplying, Mr. Bung did all in his power to coax on the

and Bung will not have such a rush of applicants to be

The present amended Bill, when it is passed into law,
will be an item to the credit of “‘ the floor of the House.”
‘Of course, if we had our own House, we could manage
these things much more satisfactorily, and with greater
despatch ; but as we haven’t, and as we want helps
to Temperance in Ireland, it is to the good that through

mons’’ we are enabled to have Bung’s wings trimmed,
if not clipped. Would it not be well for* Bung, by way
of graciously bowing to public opinion, to agree to a
Bill for the compulsory closing on St. Patrick’s Day?

ON THE WAY TO THE seilg.

“The grass upon my father’s fields
TIs green for me no more.”

T was half-past four on a summer Sunday afternoon.

I Leaving the little red brick station, with its wheeling,

- twittering swallows, its ono yawning porter, and its
~ general air of “ the world forgetting, by the world for-
.got,”” a man and woman turned to faco the white road
that rose at the right hand. It led through rolling
stretches of woodland and meadow, past tidy cottages,
a fow trim villas, and, now and again, stately homes
-engirt with smooth lawns and spreading trees. Steeped
 in sunlight and soft Sabbath peaco, tho beauty of quict
road and smiling country-side this summer day was
‘something very sweet and soothing.  Theso two were

~ the only wayfarers, The man had seen it all before.
_And yef, in a way, he had nob seen it. Then, as now,

311

LEADER.

he looked only at the white road and felb that the
climb was rather stiff for him, and the day a trifle too
warm. He was a solid Dutch-built man, set heavily
on substantial feet, such as the London police are famed
for. ' A square-cut, yellowish beard, fair skin and
leavy features complefed a Batavian ensemble. Yet
he was from besido the Shannon, not the Rhine or the
Zuyder Zeo. Hearing that you were ab ﬁl'sb'amazed;
then you remembered the Palatines, and decided that
this was an obvious *throw-back’’ to some Teuton
ancestor. Not a trace of Celtic fire or passion or mys-
tery marked his very ponderous person.

Ilis wife, the woman by his side, was in clear con-

Her deep-set, grey-blue eyes, lit now with
pleasure at the unusual sight of green fields and

grazing cattle, were quick and bright, her oval
face was thin and worn-looking, her wavy
hair  dark grey, her person slender despite
tho passage of more than two score years.

Whilo the man was pufing heavily with the climb she
was breathing only quick, little sighs of sheer happiness.
All this—country road, far-stretching fields and quiet
cattle—was at once so strange and so beautifully
familiar.

“Oh, oh!” she cried, with kindling eyes, as-a turn
in the road revealed a fine field traversed by a path;
“look over. There—beyond. Oh, I could surely think
it was Jamesy Billy’s field. ’Tis the very picture of it.””

“Get away with you!” was his comment. The
Limerick expression came with heavy brusquerie from
his lips. It was a household word to her, but she heard
it now with a sense of being brushed roughly aside.
“Jamesy Billy’s field, indeed! Would you see any
trees there? ~ And where’s the ditch here?’  His
memory was photographic, as became a conscientious
policeman. TFor his wife’s imaginative powers he had
a large contempt.

She was disappointed, but she stood to watch the
green and golden glories of the field with eyes that
looked as if they had been famished. “ Well, but’’—
she urged slowly, “leave the trees out—wouldn’t you
think it was just the same? Look, now, at the wind
waving the grass, and the cloud shadows running over
it! That’s just the way it used to be.”” “Oh, come
on, come on,”’ cried the Palatine impatiently “The
tea is at five, and we’ve more than a mile before us. I
I know the way it will be. We'll be getting cold slops
and leavings. And I'd as soon have cabbage water as
cold tea.’”

She turned her face to him slowly, like one who hears
only with the outer ear. Then she walked over to the
barred gate of the field.  “I could look at that for
over,”’ she cried to herself with soft intensity. The
solid man in the roadway clicked his tongue and
snapped his fingers with vexation. *

“Wisha, my God,”” he complained, “isn’t that
woman a fright? She’d have you mad to her, so she
would. What made me carry her out at all?”’

The soft stirring of the wind among the leaves was
his only answer. But it did not turn away his wrath.
Tho trees cast no shade over the hot, white road

CLONGOWES.

“Te lanz prope nobilem
Tonse Luceriam, non cithare, decent,
Nec flos pupureus rose.”

AM a Clongownian, and eke an Irish-Irelander.

Yet I am deeply attached to my Alma Mater. To
me, as to Thomas Francis Meagher, it is a “dear old
spot.”’ I have for it all the proverbial affection we
give to a sinful and erring parent. I always think of
Clongowes as one of those delightfully wicked old ladies
whom Thackeray loved to paint. She is still attached
to the frivolities of the last century. A belle of George
IV.’s period, she still insists on dressing herself in the
transparent English muslins which were then in
fashion, and refuses even in her age to don a more
sober garment of Irish texture. She can still win a
smile from kind old Chief Baron Palles, her co-eval
admirer, and she cannot realise that the impression
created on the younger men is not exactly similar. I
would not have another mother. Yet she is a charming
old creature, and very dear to us for all her follies, and
it is because I would have her make her soul that I,
her son, write this article.

Now Clongowes, as I knew her, was not, perhaps,
quite as bad as the Clongowes of Thomas Francis
Meagher. The current Clongownian quotes him as
saying :—“ They talked to us about Mount Olympus,
and the vales of Thessaly, paralysed us with Euclid,
pitched us precipitately into England, amidst the im-
petuous Normans and stupid Saxons, but as far as Ire-
land was concerned they left us, like blind and crippled
children, in the dark. They never spoke of Ireland;

- never gave us even what is left of her—her history—to

read ; never quickened the young, bright life they con-
trolled into lofty conceptions by a reference to the
martyrdoms, the wrongs, the soldiership, the statesman-
ship, the magnificent memories, and illuminating hopes
of the poor old land.”

Matters have improved since that date, and
I have often heard the West-Britonism of Clon-
gowes cxaggerated. A certain amount of the more
modern Irish history was studied. I have heard tho
famous passage of Meagher about the “Sword”’ read
while I was at school. A huge veneration for O’Con-
nell was handed down tra,dibiona,llf' in the college. Jesuif
Fathers were ever oager to tell a circle of listening
scholars how they confessed the Fenians in the College
Chapel when they could not get absolution olsewhere.
Treland was nob exactly treated as an English province,
as they seem to deal with it in Trinity ; it was rather pre-
sented in the light of an Anglicised quasi-country ~Aban-
doning everything native, it was to draw its intellect
from ancient Greece, and its amusements from modern
Eton, To substitute the cheap and Catholic “ Clong.”
for the expensive and Protestant “Et.”” in the word
Etonian was its unfulfilled aspiration. To be Irish,

" but Irish vory much after the fashion of O’Connell,

an Ireland made up of jobs and ‘judges and soldiers,
has hitherto been the Clongowes tone. I am an Irish- *
man, but I am every bit as good an Englishman as you
are ’’—I think that was at the back of a good many
Clongowes heads. Both tendencies took effect, and the
Irishman in the Clongownian became Thomas Francis
Meagher, the Englishman developed in his brother
Colonel Henry Meagher, who is recorded as having
onded an inglorious caréer as a militia officer during the
past year!

Clongowes has, in some respects, a difficult part to
play. The abominable system of sending Irish lads to
English schools puts her on her West British mettle.
She fears that if the green flag is seen flying over her.
grounds, she will lose her more respectable element, and
the higher bourgeoisic will send their children across the
wadter, and she may also lose the children of the Connaught
gentry, who are still faithful to her. Hence the cult
of the cricket-bat, and the dread of the camdn. The
Irish-Society-fed mother loves respectability, and thinks
that to be English is to be respectable, and the Rector
has a rooted dread of mothers. Yet Hurley is, above
all things, the treatment needed for Clongowes’ health,
and when it adopts it, its recovery will be fast and cer-
tain. 'What a title of shame is that name, the
“cradle of Irish cricketers,”” which is claimed for the
school in the current Clongownian; what shame this .
glorying in a dishonourable off-spring. Again, what
bitter, if unintentional, sarcasm there is in that phrase
with which the article entitled “ Hockey, 1906,” begins.
“ Hockey is a game which grows yearly more popular
at Clongowes.  Is it because it resembles so closely
hurling ?”’

Why does not Clongowes play hurley? She need not
stop cricket at present. She may not be able to give
up sin all at once, but why not endeavour to merit in
the meantime? After a time reform will become easier.
Many of the boys from the South must know hurley
well, and many a boy would find pleasure in it, who at
present groans beneath the tyranny of cricket? Men’s
pleasures are diverse, yet in summer it is cricket or nothing
(save just a little tennis) with the Clongowes boy. ‘Cricket
is a game quite unsuited to the Irish temperament; the
average Irishman differs from the average Briton in
that he was no more born a cricketer than born a
coward in battle. Again, in Clongowes they play in
the mornings an abominable game called gravel foot-
ball, once a French game, but now become a debased
form of association. Nine men out of ten detest it,
and were hurley substituted for it, there would be
almost universal joy. But once again, there are the
Irish-Society mothers.  Well, if they are an obstacle.
send round circulars asking whether Irish games are
disapproved of, and let the chosen ones be kept free
from contamination. As to hurley being dangerous,
that is, of course, nonsense.

But now, in the matter of mothers, a word in your
ear. I know something of what the mothers of
the higher bourgeoisie think of Clongowes, and it‘is not:
about the Irish language they complain. It is not in.
frequent in this country to seek to compensate for a
deficiency in cleanliness by a deficiency in patriotism.
In the terrible matter of tidiness and manners, is the.
Clongowes boy above reproach. I don’t, of course,
suggest that he is worse in this respect than boys at
other Irish schools. I am sure he is better. The Black-
rocker is in these matters—well, I will be piously
silont.  But is tho Clongownian ever an example
of matter in its right place, which is tidiness, and
matter in its conventional place, which is manners?¥
The mothers say not, and draw scathing comparisons,
with English schools. I would not like to express a
dofinite opinion, but I believe that if the students’
boots were blacked instead of greased, these two virtues.
would be much promoted. In my time everyone lived,
down to his boots, and no one could have any heart in
neat dressing, when he came ultimately to so uglya nether
conclusion. And very few did try to dress neatly.
Nor is this seemingly entirely changed. Look at the
frontispieco. It depicts the exhibitioners dressed, we.
may presume, in their best. The first boy has his tie.
awry. The right-hand boy in the second row is still
worse. Two out of the three boys below them suffer-

THE GREEN IMMORTAL TRIO.
(Air—* Oh, the Shamrock.”)

In Erin’s Isle for sport awhile
Pink, Green and Buff are pining.

The football teams that filled up reams
No longer fields are lining.

‘Where’er they pass they find the grass
In sad profusion sprouting,

Where oafs mud-splashed, but lately dashed,
And set them gladly shouting—

Ob, the football, the green immortal football,
The thing to puff Pink, Green and Buff

Is Ireland’s British football.

Said Pink, “ I'm sick, and want a kick
Of British boot so bracing,’’
Said Green, “ the same would suit my game
Like trolley tricks and racing.”’
““Look here,”” said Buff, “a tussle rough
‘With Soccer knickers sprawling,
The ha’pence brown my coffers down
‘Would send like hailstones falling.”’
Oh, the football, the green immortal football, etc.

““ Some months, alas!’’ they sighed, “ must pass
Before the leather flying,
’Mid cheers and yells proclaims the Shells,
And other teams are trying.
The Shells are bricks for giving kicks
That wake our half-backs sleeping,
And coppers roll into the goal
Pink, Green and Buff are keeping.’’
Oh, the football, the green immortal football, etc.

The bookies still our sheets may fill
With betting tips surprising,
‘While Sapho plays and Kitty Greys
May want some advertising.
Sensations new and scandals, too,
Are mighty good gap-stoppers,
But nothing licks the football kicks
For bringing in the coppers.
Oh, the football, the green immortal football, etc.

A. M. W.

HAVE proved that the giving of 36 Exhibitions,
] worth £260 a year to Trinity College, has beon a
breach of trust; that the Act of 1723 has not legalised
the giving of these Exhibitions; that the Act of 1723
has only given the Governors, or Trustees, power to dis-
pose of an alleged surplus; that it has dofined that sur-
plus to bo what remains after all the Trusts reposed in
the Trustees have been fulfilled ; and that as the Endow-
ment falls far short of fulfilling all the Trusts reposed
in the Trustees, there is not, and there cannot be, a
surplus as defined by the Act. And hence I have proved
that all the money given away under the Act of 1723
has been given away without legal authority, and by an
egregious breach of trust.

316 THE LEADER.

Jury 7, 1906.

The Act says:— Therefore, for the better enabling
the Governors of the said schools . to apply the
surplus rents of the said lands . . .
which shall remain after payment of the sevpra.l yearly
sums already appointed as aforesaid, and all
other necessary charges in and about the execcution of
the Trusts reposed in them to the uses herein men-
tioned.”” Thus the Governors had got legal power to
apply to all and every purpose mentioned in the Act of
1723, only what remained, after all the Trusts reposed
in them had been fulfilled. And, as nothing could
remain after all the Trusts reposed in them had been
fulfilled, they got legal power to apply to the uses men-
tioned in the Act of 1723 a sum of money which did
not, and could not, exist. And thus are swept away,
at one stroke, all the purposes mentioned in the Act of
1723, and to which the Governors have, without a par-
ticle of legal authority, given some £600,000" of the
Endowment. They havo given this money to Trinity
College, to Bluecoat Hospital, to the English schools, to
the Ennis Grammar School, to Brunswick Street School,
and to Harcourt Street School. Let us see what they
have dono for Trinity College.

Not only did they falsely tell the Legislature that
they had fulfilled all the Trusts reposed in them, and
had left on hand a surplus, but they also falsely told
them that it would be  greatly for the service of the
publick ” to establish two Lectureships and three Junior
Fellowships in Trinity College. It could not be for the
public service to violate a Trust, and to take away from
6,000 children the money to which they were entitled,
and to give it to Trinity College already endowed with
200,000 acres of stolen lands.

The Act enacts that three Junior Fellowships be
established in Trinity College from the first of May,
1724 ; and that each of the Junior Fellows should be
“ paid the yearly sum of £33 6s. 8d. out of the surplus
rents of the said lands.”” The Legislature could, of
course, establish the Fellowships, but as there were no
surplus rents, there was nothing to pay the Fellows. It
is true that the Act afterwards provides that even
though the rents should decrease or grow less, that the
sums payable to Junior Fellows “shall continue and
remain payable to them.”’

Now, we cannot interpret or construe the Act, so as
to make one part of it contradict another part.  And
since one part of the Act has clearly directed that the
salaries of the Junior Fellows are to be paid out of the
surplus—and this statement is, moreover, confirmed by
the fact that the Legislature was only asked to give
legal power to dispose of a surplus—we cannot take
another-part of it, as directing the very opposite, that
those salaries should be paid out of money which is not
a surplus. If we adopted this latter view, we should
take the Act as not only contradicting itself, but as
giving more legal power than had been asked for. We
must, therefore, take the latter quotation directing
what is to be done when the rents have fallen, as having
reference to a state of things in which, even after the
rents have fallen, there still remains a surplus. And as
the direction of the Act in this state of things is, that
the salaries of the Junior Fellows should be paid out of
a surplus, when there is no surplus, they cannot be paid.

Scholars of the House were to be preferred for those
Fellowships; and after them, any who veceived an
Erasmus Smith Exhibition. He needed not to have
been the son of a tenant or inhabitant on the lands,
nor to have been educated in the Erasmus Smith Schools.

The Act of 1723 next directs that two public Lecture-
ships be established in Trinity Collego from and after
the 1st of May, 1724—that is to say, one in Oratory and
History, and the other in Natural and Experimental
Philosophy, at salaries of £35 a year, to ba paid to each
‘“out of the Surplus Rents and Profits.”” The candi-
dates were to bo selected by public examination, and
were to be approved of, and appointed by the Governors
of the Brasmus Smith Schools, “for such time only as
they should appoint by instruments under their Common
